course_info.title: Instructional Design
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 1 hour per session

class_schedule.topic: The ADDIE Model and Systematic Design
learning_objectives: Describe the five phases of the ADDIE model
Explain why systematic instructional design improves learning outcomes
Compare ADDIE with agile and rapid prototyping approaches
readings: Dick, Carey & Carey - The Systematic Design of Instruction Ch. 1-2
Molenda, M. - In Search of the Elusive ADDIE Model

lecture_notes.introduction: Instructional design is the engineering discipline of education. Just as architects do not build without blueprints, instructional designers do not create learning experiences without systematic analysis and planning.
key_concepts: ADDIE: Analysis, Design, Development, Implementation, Evaluation
Systems approach to instruction
Front-end analysis: the foundation of good design
Iterative vs. waterfall models of ID
Agile instructional design and rapid prototyping
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a complete mini-ADDIE cycle for a 15-minute training module on password security

review.value: A framework, not a rigid recipe: Analysis identifies the problem, Design plans the solution, Development builds it, Implementation delivers it, Evaluation measures its effectiveness
Successive approximation model (SAM) uses rapid prototyping and iterative cycles to produce design artifacts faster while maintaining instructional quality

learning_objectives: Conduct a needs analysis to identify performance gaps
Develop learner profiles using demographic and psychographic data
Distinguish between instructional and non-instructional solutions

lecture_notes.introduction: The most elegant instruction in the world is useless if it solves the wrong problem. Needs analysis ensures we invest design effort where it will make a real difference.
key_concepts: Gap analysis: current state vs. desired state
Performance analysis: is training the right solution?
Learner analysis: prior knowledge, attitudes, demographics
Context analysis: learning and performance environments
Data collection methods: surveys, interviews, observation, document review
lecture_notes.demonstration: Practice needs analysis interview: role-play a client interview to surface the real performance problem behind a training request

review.key: Not Every Problem Is a Training Problem
review.value: If employees lack motivation, tools, or clear expectations, training alone will not improve performance. Analysis must distinguish knowledge/skill gaps from environmental barriers.
Understanding prior knowledge is the single most important factor in designing effective instruction (Ausubel): what the learner already knows determines how new information is processed

learning_objectives: Write measurable learning objectives using the ABCD format
Align objectives to appropriate levels of Bloom's taxonomy
Distinguish between terminal and enabling objectives
readings: Anderson & Krathwohl - A Taxonomy for Learning, Teaching, and Assessing (overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: Clear learning objectives are the foundation of aligned instruction. Without them, you cannot design appropriate activities, and you certainly cannot assess whether learning occurred.
key_concepts: ABCD format: Audience, Behavior, Condition, Degree
Bloom's revised taxonomy: Remember through Create
Terminal objectives vs. enabling objectives
Cognitive, affective, and psychomotor domains
Alignment: objectives drive assessment and instruction
lecture_notes.demonstration: Objective repair workshop: rewrite five vague objectives (e.g., 'understand photosynthesis') into measurable ABCD format at specified Bloom's levels

review.value: Audience (who), Behavior (observable action verb), Condition (under what circumstances), Degree (how well): 'Given a dataset (C), the student (A) will calculate the mean and standard deviation (B) with 100% accuracy (D)'
Objectives, assessments, and instructional activities must all target the same knowledge and cognitive level; misalignment is the most common design flaw

learning_objectives: Design assessment items aligned to specific learning objectives
Distinguish between formative and summative assessment strategies
Apply principles of validity and reliability to assessment design

lecture_notes.introduction: Assessment is not an afterthought added to the end of a course. In backward design, assessment items are written immediately after objectives, before instruction is developed. This ensures that what you measure matches what you intend to teach.
key_concepts: Backward design: objectives, then assessments, then instruction
Formative assessment: check understanding during learning
Summative assessment: evaluate achievement at the end
Item types: selected response, constructed response, performance-based
Validity and reliability in assessment design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Assessment alignment exercise: given three objectives at different Bloom's levels, design one assessment item for each that validly measures the target behavior
setup_requirements: Sample objectives at varying Bloom's levels

review.value: Wiggins & McTighe: identify desired results, determine acceptable evidence, then plan learning experiences. Assessment design precedes activity design.
Low-stakes checks during instruction (polls, exit tickets, concept checks) that provide feedback to both instructor and learner for real-time adjustment

learning_objectives: Select instructional strategies appropriate to the learning objective type
Apply Gagné's Nine Events of Instruction to lesson design
Choose delivery media based on instructional requirements and constraints
readings: Gagné, R. - The Conditions of Learning (events overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: Once you know what to teach and how to assess it, you need to decide how to teach it. Instructional strategy selection should be driven by the type of learning outcome, not by personal preference or available technology.
key_concepts: Gagné's Nine Events of Instruction
Strategies by outcome type: verbal information, intellectual skills, motor skills, attitudes
Direct instruction, guided discovery, problem-based learning
Media selection frameworks: not 'what's trendy' but 'what fits'
Merrill's First Principles: problem-centered, activation, demonstration, application, integration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Lesson architecture exercise: design a 30-minute lesson using Gagné's Nine Events, then identify which media best supports each event

review.value: Gain attention, inform objectives, stimulate recall, present content, provide guidance, elicit performance, provide feedback, assess performance, enhance transfer
Learning is promoted when it is problem-centered, activates prior knowledge, demonstrates the skill, lets learners apply it, and integrates it into their world

learning_objectives: Apply Kirkpatrick's four levels of evaluation to an instructional program
Design evaluation instruments for reaction, learning, behavior, and results
Critique the limitations of Kirkpatrick's model and describe alternatives
readings: Kirkpatrick, D. & Kirkpatrick, J. - Evaluating Training Programs (excerpts)

lecture_notes.introduction: Evaluation closes the loop in instructional design. Without it, you are guessing whether your instruction worked. This session covers the most widely used evaluation framework and its alternatives.
key_concepts: Kirkpatrick's four levels: Reaction, Learning, Behavior, Results
Formative evaluation: expert review, one-to-one, small group, field trial
Summative evaluation: effectiveness and efficiency
ROI analysis: level 5 (Phillips)
Limitations: correlation is not causation, attribution challenges
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluation plan design: create a complete evaluation plan for a corporate onboarding program, specifying instruments and data collection at each Kirkpatrick level
setup_requirements: Sample program description for evaluation design

review.value: Level 1 (Reaction: did they like it?), Level 2 (Learning: did they learn it?), Level 3 (Behavior: do they use it on the job?), Level 4 (Results: did business outcomes improve?)
Conducted during development to improve the instruction before full deployment; expert review catches content errors, learner tryouts catch usability and comprehension issues

quizzes.topic: ADDIE, Needs Analysis, and Learning Objectives

questions.question: A company's sales team is underperforming. Management requests a training program on sales techniques. As an instructional designer, what analysis would you conduct before agreeing to design training, and what alternative solutions might you consider?
questions.answer: Conduct a performance analysis to determine whether the gap is due to a lack of knowledge/skills (training solution) or environmental factors like unclear expectations, inadequate tools, poor incentives, or insufficient feedback (non-training solutions). Methods: interview top and poor performers, review sales processes, analyze CRM data, observe the sales environment. Alternatives to training: revised compensation structure, better CRM tools, clearer performance expectations, coaching/mentoring, or process redesign.

rubric.key: Identifies need for performance analysis before training

questions.question: Rewrite this vague objective into proper ABCD format at the Application level of Bloom's taxonomy: 'Students will understand project management.'
questions.answer: Given a project scenario with defined scope, timeline, and resources (Condition), the student (Audience) will create a work breakdown structure and Gantt chart that accounts for all deliverables and dependencies (Behavior) with no more than two scheduling errors (Degree).

rubric.key: All four ABCD components present and labeled
Observable behavior verb at Application level

questions.question: Explain the difference between formative and summative evaluation in instructional design. At what point in the design process should each occur, and give a specific example of each.
questions.answer: Formative evaluation occurs during development to improve the instruction before full deployment. Example: conducting a one-to-one tryout where a single learner works through the draft materials while the designer observes and notes confusion points. Summative evaluation occurs after implementation to judge the overall effectiveness of the finished instruction. Example: comparing pre-test and post-test scores of learners who completed the program, then measuring on-the-job transfer at 30 and 90 days.

questions.question: List Gagné's Nine Events of Instruction and explain how Event 6 (Elicit Performance) differs from Event 8 (Assess Performance). Why is the distinction important?
questions.answer: The nine events: (1) Gain attention, (2) Inform learners of objectives, (3) Stimulate recall of prior learning, (4) Present content, (5) Provide learning guidance, (6) Elicit performance, (7) Provide feedback, (8) Assess performance, (9) Enhance retention and transfer. Event 6 (Elicit Performance) is practice with support — the learner attempts the skill and receives corrective feedback (Event 7). Event 8 (Assess Performance) is a formal, unsupported evaluation of whether the learner can perform independently. The distinction matters because learners need guided practice before being assessed; skipping practice leads to poor assessment results and learner frustration.

rubric.key: Explains why the distinction matters for design

major_assignments.title: Complete Instructional Design Document
major_assignments.description: Design a complete one-hour instructional module on a topic of your choice. Produce a full design document that includes needs analysis findings, learner analysis, learning objectives, aligned assessments, an instructional strategy with Gagné's events mapped, media specifications, and an evaluation plan using Kirkpatrick's model.
objectives: Apply the complete ADDIE process to a real instructional problem
Write measurable objectives aligned to assessments and instruction
Select and justify instructional strategies based on learning outcome types
deliverables: Instructional strategy flowchart with Gagné's events

criteria: Clear identification of the performance gap
Justified decision that instruction is the appropriate solution
Well-written ABCD objectives at appropriate Bloom's levels
Assessments validly aligned to each objective
Instructional strategy justified by learning outcome type
All nine of Gagné's events addressed
Media selections justified by instructional requirements
Strong alignment among objectives, assessments, and strategies
Professional quality suitable for a client deliverable

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on instructional design concepts and processes

lab_assignments.description: Weekly ID practice exercises: needs analysis, objective writing, assessment design

midterm_examination.description: Covers ADDIE, needs analysis, objectives, and assessment design

final_project.description: Develop and pilot-test a prototype instructional module based on the design document

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused lab absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day, up to 5 days. No submissions accepted after 5 days without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review is an integral part of the design process and is encouraged. All design documents must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Proper citation of all sources is required. AI-generated text must be disclosed. Violations result in a zero and academic integrity referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: A computer with internet access and word processing software. Access to presentation or prototyping tools for the final project.
